learning_objectives: Assess the current threat landscape targeting healthcare organizations
Understand why healthcare is a prime target for cyberattacks
Identify the consequences of healthcare data breaches
readings: Healthcare Information Security and Privacy Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare is the most breached industry in the United States, with the average breach costing $10.9 million. Understanding the threat landscape is the first step to defending it.
key_concepts: Healthcare breach statistics: frequency, cost, impact on patient safety
Threat actors: nation-states, organized crime, insiders, hacktivists
Attack vectors: phishing, ransomware, supply chain, medical device exploitation
Consequences: patient safety risk, operational disruption, financial penalties, reputational damage
The intersection of cybersecurity and patient safety
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of the 2020 Universal Health Services ransomware attack: timeline, impact, response, and lessons learned
setup_requirements: Access to HHS Breach Portal for case studies
Course lab environment with virtual machines

review.value: Healthcare breaches cost an average of $10.9M per incident (IBM 2023), the highest of any industry for 13 consecutive years
Healthcare ransomware attacks divert ambulances, delay surgeries, and have been linked to increased patient mortality

learning_objectives: Analyze each HIPAA Security Rule standard in detail
Map security controls to HIPAA requirements
Conduct a HIPAA security risk assessment
readings: Healthcare Information Security and Privacy Ch. 4
NIST SP 800-66: Implementing the HIPAA Security Rule

lecture_notes.introduction: The HIPAA Security Rule is the regulatory foundation for healthcare cybersecurity. Every security professional in healthcare must know it thoroughly and be able to implement its requirements.
key_concepts: Administrative safeguards: risk analysis, workforce training, incident response, contingency planning
Physical safeguards: facility access, workstation security, device and media controls
Technical safeguards: access control, audit controls, integrity controls, transmission security
Required vs. addressable implementation specifications
OCR enforcement: investigation process, civil monetary penalties, resolution agreements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a HIPAA security risk assessment using the HHS SRA tool, identifying gaps in a sample organization

review.value: Required specifications must be implemented. Addressable specifications must be assessed; if not implemented, the organization must document why and implement an equivalent alternative
The foundational HIPAA requirement: systematic identification and assessment of potential risks to ePHI confidentiality, integrity, and availability

learning_objectives: Design network segmentation for a hospital environment
Implement defense-in-depth strategies for healthcare networks
Evaluate firewall, IDS/IPS, and endpoint protection solutions
readings: Healthcare Information Security and Privacy Ch. 7
NIST Cybersecurity Framework Healthcare Implementation Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Hospital networks are uniquely complex: medical devices, guest WiFi, clinical workstations, and IoT sensors share infrastructure. Securing this environment requires layered defenses.
key_concepts: Network segmentation: clinical, administrative, medical device, guest VLANs
Defense-in-depth: firewall, IDS/IPS, NAC, endpoint protection, DNS filtering
Medical device network isolation and monitoring
Zero trust architecture principles applied to healthcare
Wireless security for clinical environments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a network segmentation plan for a 200-bed hospital, identifying VLANs, firewall rules, and medical device isolation requirements
setup_requirements: Virtual lab environment with firewall and network simulator

review.value: Isolating medical devices on dedicated VLANs with restricted communication paths because many devices run legacy OS versions and cannot be patched
Security model requiring verification for every access request regardless of network location - 'never trust, always verify'

class_schedule.topic: Identity and Access Management in Healthcare
learning_objectives: Design role-based and attribute-based access control for clinical systems
Manage privileged access and break-the-glass scenarios
readings: Healthcare Information Security and Privacy Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare presents unique IAM challenges: clinicians need fast access in emergencies, patients move between departments, and hundreds of applications require single sign-on.
key_concepts: RBAC for clinical systems: role definitions aligned with clinical responsibilities
ABAC: attribute-based access adding context (location, time, patient relationship)
Single sign-on (SSO) with tap-and-go badge authentication
Break-the-glass: emergency access with mandatory post-access audit
Privileged access management: admin accounts, service accounts, vendor access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configure RBAC policies for an EHR: physician, nurse, pharmacist, coder, and patient roles with appropriate access levels
setup_requirements: EHR training environment for access control testing

review.value: Emergency access mechanism allowing providers to access records outside their normal authorization scope, with logging and mandatory justification review
Single Sign-On with proximity badge (tap-and-go) enabling clinicians to authenticate quickly across workstations, reducing password burden

learning_objectives: Develop an incident response plan for a healthcare organization
Execute breach assessment and notification procedures under HIPAA
readings: Healthcare Information Security and Privacy Ch. 11
NIST SP 800-61: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: When a breach occurs, the response determines the difference between a contained incident and a catastrophe. Healthcare organizations must be prepared with tested incident response plans.
key_concepts: Incident response phases: preparation, detection, containment, eradication, recovery, lessons learned
HIPAA breach assessment: the four-factor risk assessment for determining breach notification
Notification requirements: individual notice (60 days), HHS (60 days), media (500+ affected)
Forensic investigation: evidence preservation, chain of custody, root cause analysis
Tabletop exercises and IR plan testing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tabletop exercise: respond to a ransomware attack scenario affecting the EHR, making real-time decisions about containment, communication, and patient safety

review.value: HIPAA requires assessing: (1) nature of PHI involved, (2) who accessed it, (3) whether PHI was actually viewed, (4) extent of risk mitigation
HHS guidance (2016) presumes ransomware is a reportable breach because encryption by unauthorized actors compromises data availability

learning_objectives: Assess security risks in connected medical devices
Understand FDA premarket and postmarket cybersecurity guidance
Develop a medical device security program for a hospital
readings: Healthcare Information Security and Privacy Ch. 13
FDA Guidance: Cybersecurity in Medical Devices (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Hospitals contain thousands of connected medical devices, many running outdated operating systems with known vulnerabilities. Securing these life-critical devices is one of healthcare's greatest cybersecurity challenges.
key_concepts: Medical device threat landscape: legacy OS, default credentials, unencrypted protocols
FDA premarket cybersecurity requirements for device manufacturers
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) requirements and vulnerability management
Hospital medical device security program: inventory, risk assessment, patching, monitoring
Clinical engineering and IT security collaboration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a mock vulnerability assessment of a medical device network segment, identifying risks and recommending mitigations

review.value: Software Bill of Materials: inventory of all software components in a device, required by FDA so hospitals can identify when known vulnerabilities affect their devices
Many medical devices run Windows XP/7 or embedded Linux that cannot be updated, requiring compensating controls like network isolation and monitoring

quizzes.topic: HIPAA Security Rule and Network Security

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'required' and 'addressable' implementation specifications in the HIPAA Security Rule. Provide an example of each and describe what an organization must do if it determines an addressable specification is not reasonable.
questions.answer: Required specifications must be implemented as stated with no alternative. Example: conducting a risk analysis (45 CFR 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A)) - every covered entity must perform this. Addressable specifications must be assessed; the organization can implement as specified, implement an equivalent alternative, or not implement if neither is reasonable. Example: encryption of ePHI at rest (45 CFR 164.312(a)(2)(iv)) - if a small practice determines full encryption is not reasonable, they could implement access controls and audit logging as alternatives. If not implementing, they must document: (1) why it is not reasonable, (2) what alternative measure achieves the same purpose, and (3) the rationale for the alternative.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between required and addressable
Valid example of each with CFR reference

questions.question: Why is network segmentation particularly critical in hospital environments? Describe at least three separate network segments a hospital should maintain and justify the isolation of each.
questions.answer: Network segmentation is critical because hospitals have diverse devices with vastly different security profiles sharing physical infrastructure. A compromised device on an unsegmented network could reach everything from medical devices to billing systems. Three segments: (1) Clinical network: workstations, EHR servers, clinical printers - contains PHI and requires strict access controls. (2) Medical device VLAN: infusion pumps, ventilators, imaging equipment - many run legacy OS and cannot be patched, so isolation prevents lateral movement from compromised devices. (3) Guest/patient WiFi: internet access for visitors and patients - must be completely isolated from clinical and administrative networks to prevent unauthorized access to internal systems. Additional segments could include administrative (HR, finance), biomedical engineering, and IoT (environmental sensors, badge readers).

rubric.key: Clear rationale for segmentation in hospitals
Security justification for each segment's isolation

questions.question: Describe the 'break-the-glass' access control mechanism in healthcare EHRs. Why is it necessary, and what controls must surround it to prevent abuse?
questions.answer: Break-the-glass allows providers to access patient records outside their normal authorization scope during emergencies when the patient's assigned provider is unavailable. It is necessary because denying access during a clinical emergency could harm or kill the patient. Controls to prevent abuse: (1) The action is prominently logged with timestamp, user, patient, and stated reason. (2) The user must enter a justification reason before access is granted. (3) A hard alert notifies the user that the access is being monitored. (4) Privacy/compliance team reviews all break-the-glass events within 24-48 hours. (5) Unjustified access triggers investigation and potential sanctions. (6) Break-the-glass rates are tracked as a KPI - high rates may indicate role-based access is too restrictive.

rubric.key: At least 4 controls to prevent abuse identified

questions.question: A hospital discovers that an employee accessed the medical records of 600 patients without authorization over a 3-month period. Walk through the HIPAA breach assessment and notification steps.
questions.answer: Breach assessment using the four-factor test: (1) Nature of PHI: likely includes names, diagnoses, medications, and other sensitive clinical data - high risk. (2) Unauthorized person: an internal employee without a treatment, payment, or operations purpose - known individual but insider threat. (3) Whether PHI was actually acquired or viewed: access logs confirm records were opened and viewed - confirmed access. (4) Risk mitigation: employee can be terminated and attested that data was not further disclosed, but 600 records over 3 months suggests deliberate snooping. Assessment conclusion: this is a presumed breach unless the four-factor assessment demonstrates low probability of compromise. Notification requirements: (1) Individual notification to all 600 affected patients within 60 days of discovery via first-class mail. (2) HHS notification within 60 days (since >500 individuals). (3) Media notification to prominent media outlets in the state since >500 residents affected. (4) Document the investigation, assessment, and response in the breach log.

rubric.key: All notification requirements identified with timelines

major_assignments.title: Healthcare Security Risk Assessment and Remediation Plan
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive HIPAA security risk assessment for a simulated healthcare organization, document findings, and develop a prioritized remediation plan with security control recommendations.
objectives: Apply HIPAA Security Rule requirements to a realistic scenario
Conduct systematic risk identification and assessment
Prioritize risks and develop actionable remediation plans
Design security controls appropriate for healthcare environments
deliverables: Risk assessment report (15-20 pages) covering all HIPAA safeguard categories
Risk register with likelihood, impact, and risk scores
Prioritized remediation plan with timelines and resource estimates

criteria: Prioritization based on risk level and feasibility
Quick wins and long-term initiatives balanced

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of security concepts, HIPAA requirements, and threat knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on labs in network security, IAM configuration, and incident response

major_assignments.description: Healthcare security risk assessment and remediation plan

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of HIPAA Security Rule and network security

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including IAM, incident response, and medical device security

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Missing more than 2 labs without prior arrangement results in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Lab reports due within one week of the lab session.
course_policies.collaboration: Labs may be completed in teams of 2. Risk assessment and all written deliverables are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Security lab exercises must not be used against production systems. Ethical hacking principles must be followed at all times.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop capable of running virtual machines. VPN access to lab environment will be provided. Familiarity with Linux command line expected.
